prompt: A lymph node is partly cut off at the top of the image. Which control should be addressed first?

options.label: Increase depth so the target is fully framed
options.rationale: Correct. Framing comes before fine brightness or measurement decisions.

options.label: Increase gain so the clipped node looks brighter
options.rationale: Incorrect. Brightness cannot fix anatomy that is not fully in the field.

options.label: Freeze the frame to inspect the border
options.rationale: Incorrect. Freezing too early only preserves a poorly framed image.

options.label: Place calipers before changing the view
options.rationale: Incorrect. Measurement is unreliable when the target is not even fully visualized.

explanation: EBUS image rescue begins with framing. Once the target is entirely visible, gain and contrast changes become much more meaningful.

prompt: Which statement best captures the practical tradeoff between RP-EBUS and CP-EBUS?

options.label: RP-EBUS usually favors higher-resolution peripheral localization, while CP-EBUS provides deeper imaging and real-time needle guidance
options.rationale: Correct. That is the clinically useful way to remember the resolution-versus-penetration and guidance tradeoff.

options.label: CP-EBUS always gives both better penetration and better resolution than RP-EBUS
options.rationale: Incorrect. The modalities make different tradeoffs rather than one being universally superior.

options.label: RP-EBUS is mainly preferred because it has Doppler and can guide transbronchial needle aspiration in real time
options.rationale: Incorrect. Doppler and real-time TBNA are key strengths of CP-EBUS, not RP-EBUS.

options.label: The two systems differ only in screen layout and not in ultrasound behavior
options.rationale: Incorrect. Their frequency, geometry, penetration, and procedural roles differ substantially.

explanation: A useful teaching shorthand is that RP-EBUS is a higher-resolution localization tool for peripheral work, while CP-EBUS gives deeper imaging, Doppler, and real-time sampling for mediastinal and hilar targets.

prompt: Select the findings that should push the learner toward artifact rather than true anatomy.

options.label: A bright interface produces repeated equally spaced echoes that move with the reflector
options.rationale: Correct. That pattern fits reverberation rather than a true layered structure.

options.label: A dark region disappears after the probe contact improves and the angle changes
options.rationale: Correct. A finding that vanishes with coupling and angle change should raise artifact suspicion.

options.label: A vascular structure lights up on Doppler exactly where grayscale suggested a vessel
options.rationale: Incorrect. That makes a real vessel more likely, not an artifact.

options.label: A suspected border remains stable despite contact changes and is supported across multiple views
options.rationale: Incorrect. Stability across views argues more for true anatomy than for artifact.

explanation: Artifacts often behave like reflections: they track with an interface, change with angle, and disappear when coupling improves. True anatomy is usually more stable across small technique adjustments.

prompt: Put the workflow in the most defensible order for a target that may need sampling.

options.label: Set depth and framing
options.rationale: This belongs first because you need the target and surrounding anatomy in view.

options.label: Tune gain and contrast
options.rationale: This comes after framing because edge quality is easier to judge once the image geometry makes sense.

options.label: Check the intended tract with Doppler
options.rationale: This precedes puncture and measurement because it is a safety step.

options.label: Freeze and measure only if useful
options.rationale: This should happen after the image is trustworthy and after the vessel check.

explanation: A reproducible sequence reduces random button-pushing: frame first, optimize the grayscale image second, perform Doppler safety review third, and freeze or measure only when the image is truly worth preserving.

prompt: A large node has a dark avascular center and a more solid peripheral rim. After Doppler confirms a vessel-free tract, what is the best next teaching move?
caseSummary: The easiest line crosses no flow but points directly into the darkest part of the node.

options.label: Aim for the viable solid rim and fan through more than one representative area
options.rationale: Correct. A conspicuous necrotic center may be low-yield; viable tissue is a better target.

options.label: Sample the center repeatedly because it is easiest to visualize
options.rationale: Incorrect. Ease of visualization does not make an avascular necrotic center the best target.

options.label: Ignore the grayscale target because Doppler is negative
options.rationale: Incorrect. Doppler helps with safety, but grayscale still guides what part of the node is worth sampling.

options.label: Increase gain until the dark center looks more solid before puncturing
options.rationale: Incorrect. Artificially brightening the frame does not convert necrotic tissue into a higher-yield target.

explanation: Doppler helps exclude vessels, but target selection inside the node still matters. When a node is heterogeneous, sample viable tissue and use fanning rather than repeatedly traversing an obvious necrotic cavity.

prompt: What is the best reason to turn Doppler on before an EBUS-TBNA pass?

options.label: To identify flow in adjacent vessels and refine a safe puncture path
options.rationale: Correct. That is the highest-yield practical use before sampling.

options.label: To replace grayscale interpretation of the node architecture
options.rationale: Incorrect. Doppler complements grayscale; it does not replace it.

options.label: To improve the node border the same way contrast does
options.rationale: Incorrect. Doppler provides flow information, not grayscale edge separation.

options.label: To determine the final diagnosis without tissue
options.rationale: Incorrect. Doppler can refine suspicion and safety, but tissue remains definitive.

explanation: Doppler is mainly a safety and target-selection tool in this context. It helps confirm whether the intended tract crosses vascular structures and whether a suspicious area is avascular or vascular.
